task: Identify the makers of the four antique cast-iron desk seal presses in the user-supplied photograph.

config.note: Python pipeline not executed; .env absent. Grok rate-limited, Claude filled the Grok slot. Round 2 dropped all Grampp-derived sources per user directive.

phases.round_1_summary: Fan-out across Grampp-derived secondary sources produced candidate list that ChatGPT had already partially provided. Identified the form as screw-type C-scroll + mushroom-dome knob.

round_2_reconnaissance.claude_r1_summary: Pivot to USPTO primary records + numismatic scholarship. Every documented US seal-press patent (1843-1907) describes a DIFFERENT mechanism from the pictured form.

primary_patents_audited.corrects_grampp: officemuseum.com claimed this was a screw press; patent text contradicts

primary_patents_audited.patentee: John C. Merriam + William N. Weeden, Boston

round_2_reconnaissance.conclusion: No US utility patent matches the photographed form. Combined with CastIronCollector.com's attribution epistemology ('small 19th-c. foundries usually put no markings as to maker'), the form is confirmed as a non-patented commodity casting.

threads.hypothesis: Zero US utility patents match the pictured screw+mushroom+C-scroll form
Lowell & Senter is the only named seal-press exhibitor at 1839 MCMA Boston
Merriam -> Weeden partnership -> Brigham successor -> Attleboro lineage is primary-source-verified
Fraser 1843 patent was wrongly categorized as screw press by officemuseum/Grampp
Carsley D3,363 proves USPTO issued design patents for press bodies; absence of D-patent match for the C-scroll form suggests pre-1842 or European-pattern public domain
Pictured C-scroll screw press form is a non-patented commodity casting - no single attributable maker
Skinner/Bonhams, Cowan's, Christie's list unmarked C-scroll desk presses only as generic 'American cast-iron seal press, late 19th c.' without maker attribution

new_primary_findings: US 3,127 (Fraser 1843) is a LEVER press in a hollow cylinder, NOT a screw press - contradicts officemuseum.com/Grampp categorization
US 56,244 (Merriam-Weeden 1866) is a PERCUSSION press, gives real names: John C. Merriam + William N. Weeden of Boston
Weeden (1841-1891): Merriam's partner c.1866 onwards; specialized in Masonic medals (1867 Washington Boston Masonic Temple Medal; 1867 Dame Grandmaster Medal); died New Bedford 1891
c.1875: Joseph H. Merriam sold the firm to Clarence J. Brigham, who operated briefly as Brigham & Co. in Boston before relocating to Attleboro, MA
US 227,522 (1880) is a pocket lever press by J. Morris Harding of Philadelphia - NOT related to 'Evens & Watson Philadelphia' hallucination
US 344,829 (1886) by John Henry Hamilton of Concord NH is a lead-seal freight press, not a desk embosser
1839 MCMA 2nd Exhibition (Quincy Hall, 23 Sep-4 Oct): 15,000 exhibits, 70,000 visitors; Lowell & Senter the only named seal-press exhibitor; judges said 'suitable for all public offices'

CastIronCollector: small 19th-c. foundries typically did not mark their work because they served local markets where provenance was known socially - explains absence of maker's marks on commodity presses

round_2_synthesis.final_verdict: No single named maker can be identified from the photograph. The four presses are an industrial-ecosystem commodity casting, not a branded product.
round_2_synthesis.confidence_level: HIGH (on the impossibility of image-only attribution)

best_candidates: stripped cast iron of same pattern

corrections_issued: 'Evens & Watson Philadelphia' is a stove foundry, not seal press; real firm is Platt Evens Jr. Cincinnati
Illinois Iron & Bolt Company: no primary evidence for seal presses
Adam Ramage and George E. Clymer: printing press inventors, not seal press lineage
B.B. Hill patent is lever-cam, not screw press
John Fraser 1843 patent is lever-in-cylinder, not screw press (corrects officemuseum/Grampp)
round_2_synthesis.grok_blind_spot_round_2: Round 1 still over-weighted Grampp-derived sources. User was correct to flag. The impossibility of image-only attribution is itself the answer, not a research failure. Any specific maker attribution without the underside photograph is speculation.
definitive_attribution_requires: underside-of-base photograph (maker name cast, pour-hole pattern)
